pattern: Counting permutations by their inversion count is a classic DP where inserting the largest element into every gap adds a sliding window of contributions, giving a prefix-sum recurrence.
hints: Think about building the permutation one value at a time: inserting a new largest element into a slot from the right adds a known number of new inversions.
Let dp[i][j] be the count of arrangements of i numbers with exactly j inversions; the new element can add 0..i-1 inversions, so dp[i][j] sums dp[i-1][j-t] for t in that window.
Use prefix sums to collapse the inner window sum to O(1): dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] + dp[i-1][j] - dp[i-1][j-i], all modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: single number cannot have any inversion
k equals the maximum possible inversions for n
k exceeds the maximum possible inversions, answer must be zero
larger n and k requiring the prefix-sum optimization

stress.description: random n and k within constraints
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, min(size, 1000))
    k = rng.randint(0, min(size, 1000))
    return [n, k]
stress.note: exercises the modular prefix-sum DP at scale
